summary: DHH argues that imperfections and quirks in products create deeper emotional connections than polished perfection does. He draws parallels between the Porsche 911's rear engine, mechanical watches, and vintage computers to explain why he's fallen in love with the Framework 13 laptop running Linux. Despite acknowledging its drawbacks compared to modern MacBooks—inferior battery life, adequate-but-not-great screen, less polish—he finds the machine's quirky design choices and compromises endearing. He encourages programmers to try Linux, arguing it's finally good enough for desktop use, and suggests that embracing imperfection can lead to a more meaningful computing experience.
key_points: Products that achieve near-perfection paradoxically become harder to form emotional connections with, while flawed but distinctive products inspire loyalty and affection
The Framework 13 laptop recreates the quirky appeal of classic computers through its unusual 3:2 display, deep-travel keyboard, and modular interchangeable ports
Linux desktop has quietly become viable for daily use, with Ubuntu and a few extensions providing a delightful experience that's surprisingly close to macOS
Most people rightly prefer polished, frictionless tools—but for those who appreciate mechanical engagement and distinctiveness, imperfect alternatives offer something deeper
The drawbacks of the Framework 13 and Linux (battery life, screen resolution, polish) are real but ultimately don't matter much in practice
money_quotes: The engine is in wrong place in a Porsche 911. It's hanging out the back, swinging the car like a pendulum. And that's key to why it's the most iconic sports car ever made.
The mac in particular has been massaged to within an inch of perfection, and has thus become harder to connect with.
We strive to make things better and better, but if we succeed, we reminisce of the quirks that used to be.
It was so good because it was also kinda bad.
This quirky, flawed machine has created a connection I haven't had with a piece of physical computer hardware in a very long time.
I've smirked about 'This Is The Year of Linux on the Desktop' for over twenty years, but now that I've been actually running it for over a month, I've realized it's actually here.
Dare to add a little imperfection into your computing setup, and you might just find a deeper connection to the bits and electrons running it all.
key_insight: Perfection breeds indifference while imperfections create the friction and character that forge genuine emotional connections with the tools we use.
